text: In this video, a robotic right hemicolectomy with CME adopting the critical view concept is shown that was recently published by our group. In order to show all aspects with sufficient clarity, we have used footage from two patients for this video.
The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg, tilted to the right side, facilitating access to the surgical site for the robotic right hemicolectomy.
The surgeon exposes the fourth part of the duodenum and incises the peritoneum, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon follows the dorsal aspect of the duodenum in a medial to lateral direction to preserve the Toltz fascia for the retrofacial approach.
The surgeon follows the dissection plane cranially towards the dorsal aspect of the hepatic flexure and caudally to the level of the cecum, revealing the anatomical structures along this path.
The surgeon carefully applies traction and counter-traction to the mesofacial structures, ensuring they are not traumatized during the dissection.
The surgeon incises Toll's fascia along the duodenal rim to access and dissect the ventral aspect of the uncinate process, known as Frédé's space.
The fascia remains on the dorsal aspect of the mesocolon, providing protection against potential injury and preventing tumor cell spillage.
The surgeon achieves the critical view by meticulously dissecting the pancreatic head away from the mesenteric root, revealing the anatomical structures in this area.
The surgeon concludes the dorsal dissection by dividing the medial peritoneal fixation of the cecum, completing the separation of the cecum from its peritoneal attachments.
The patient is repositioned to a right side up anti-Trendelenburg position, and the robotic surgical system is redocked for continued access to the surgical site.

The surgeon identifies the V-shaped configuration formed by the ileocolic and superior mesenteric vessels, representing critical view number two, and proceeds to incise the mesentery at the angle between these vessels.

An additional grasper exerts traction on the terminal ileum through the lid of an Alexis retractor inserted in the Pfannenstiel incision, which serves as the primary access to the abdomen. This maneuver helps avoid injury to the mesocolon along the course of the ileocolic vessels.
The surgeon enters the dorsal dissection plane, providing a clear view of the pancreatic head and establishing a 180-degree axis relative to the superior mesenteric vessels.
The surgeon safely dissects the superior mesenteric vessels, preserving and excising the entire lymphatic tissue around the ileocolic pedicle and surgical trunk en bloc with the specimen.
It is noteworthy that the superior mesenteric artery in this area usually runs on the right side of the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon follows the superior mesenteric vessels cranially and performs a half circumferential resection of the lymphatic tissue. The previous dorsal dissection enhances the mobility and accessibility of the mesenteric route, aiding in the procedure.

The surgeon divides the ileocolic vein and artery between clips placed using the assist port, following the dissection that represents critical views three and four. The sequence of these critical views may vary based on the topography of the ileocolic artery and vein.
Metal clips are applied to the surgical field, ensuring both distal ends of the applicator are visible, allowing for unobstructed further dissection.

The surgeon separates the transverse mesocolic page from the mesogastric page by dividing the gastrocolic ligament, preserving the gastromental vessels. The division begins left of the midline to access the lesser sac, leaving omental adhesions to the right colon and tumor area intact. The dissection crosses the second part of the duodenum and extends into the hepatocolic ligament, reaching the dorsal dissection plane. The mesocolon is then separated from mesogastric structures along an avascular plane.

The surgeon completes the division of rhizocolic adhesions to the duodenum, demonstrating the separation of the mesogastric page retracted cranially and the transverse mesocolic page pulled caudally, as seen in critical view number six.

The surgeon dissects the right branch of the middle colic artery using an inframesocolic approach, with cranial retraction of the mesocolon. Due to limited visibility of the artery branches, a perpendicular incision is made in the mesocolon directly anterior to the superior mesenteric vein. The dissection proceeds dorsally from the former lesser sac, revealing the right branch as a horizontal vessel originating from the middle colic artery at the base of the transverse mesocolon.

The surgeon demonstrates critical view number seven by exposing the origin of the right branch of the middle colic artery to ensure adequate arterial perfusion of the remaining transverse colon. The right branch is then divided between clips using a vessel sealer.

The surgeon carefully avoids injuring the accompanying right colic vein while dividing veins running into the gastrocolic trunk of Henle from a caudal approach. The gastro-mental vein is spared due to prior isolation and separation of mesogastric structures, ensuring the integrity of the Henle trunk.

The surgeon encounters a single right superior colic vein running into the gastrocolic trunk of Henle. An instrument is carefully passed behind the vein, maintaining its integrity while navigating the surgical field.

The surgeon completes the central dissection, dividing the mesocolon and omentum while ensuring the perfusion of the remaining colon is preserved for safe anastomosis. The colon is then divided using a stapler, followed by lateral dissection.

It is important that this is done with great care because lesions of the mesocolon or even the tumor can be of considerable prognostic relevance which may override the benefit that was accomplished by the extensive central dissection. Likewise, the division of the mesoelium is a critical step for the oncological outcome. Especially if this is done intracorporeally, it has to be ensured that an adequate distance to the lymphatic drainage area of the tumor is kept.

The surgeon divides the mesoelium using a vessel sealer and the ileum with a robotic stapler. An intracorporeal side-to-side anastomosis is performed using a 60mm blue cartridge stapler. The opening is closed with two barbed sutures starting from each end of the stapler line, crossing halfway to create a two-layer suture line.
Specimens were classified as type zero A in both patients and postoperative causes were also uneventful for both. Thank you very much for watching.
